pages.text: The effect of activator concentration on the residual strength of alkali-activated ﬂy ash pastes subjected to thermal load Alaa M. Rashad a,⇑, Sayieda R. Zeedan b a Building Materials Research and Quality Control Institute, Housing & Building National Research Center, HBRC, Cairo, Egypt b Raw Building Materials Technology and Processing Research Institute, Housing & Building National Research Center, HBRC, Cairo, Egypt article info Article history: Received 8 May 2010 Received in revised form 10 December 2010 Accepted 24 December 2010 Available online 15 February 2011 Keywords: Activated ﬂy ash Activator concentrations Elevated temperature Residual compressive strength Thermal shock resistance pH value abstract This investigation reports on a comparative study of the residual compressive strength at different tem- peratures of alkali-activated ﬂy ash (AAFA) pastes chemically activated using sodium silicate with three different concentrations named 20, 30 and 40 (wt.%). The behaviour of different mixtures in conditions of rapid temperature changes was studied. Water quench test was applied to determine thermal shock resistance. The traditional Portland cement pastes were used as a reference. The temperatures ranging from 200 /C176C to 1000 /C176C with an increment of 200 /C176C has been examined. Pore solution pH and compres- sive strengths before and after exposure to elevated temperatures were determined. The various decom- position phases formed were identiﬁed using X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), differential thermal analysis (DTG) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The results indicated that ﬂy ash activated by sodium silicate is more able to resist degradation caused by exposure to elevated tem- perature than Portland cement system as its relative strengths are superior. In the hardened AAFA pastes, as activator concentration dosage increased as the relative compressive strengths and thermal shock resistance decreased. The AAFA system is able to maintain a sufﬁcient high pH to retain the passive pro- tective layer on the surface of any reinforcing steel present. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Portland cement is the primary cementitious material that is used in the most widely construction in the world. Each year, the concrete industry produces approximately 12 billion tonnes of concrete and uses about 1.6 billion tonnes of Portland cement worldwide [1]. Indeed, with the manufacture of one tonne of ce- ment approximately 0.8 tonnes of CO 2 are launched into the atmo- sphere. The cement industry accounts for 5–8% of worldwide CO 2 emission [2], the cement industry is the second largest producer of this green house gas [3]. Not only CO 2 releases from cement manufacture but also SO3 and NOx which can cause the greenhouse effect and acid rain [4]. These cause serious environmental impact. In addition to consuming considerable amounts of virgin materials (limestone and sand) and energy (energy demand about 1700– 1800 MJ/tonne clinker [5]), producing each tonne of Portland cement of which about 1.5 tonnes of raw material is needed. In addition concrete made from Portland cement is subject to certain durability problems that are difﬁcult to solve as sulphate attack, reinforcement corrosion, aggregate-alkali reaction and degradation caused by exposure to ﬁre and elevated temperature. Concrete strength begins to lose at approximately 400 /C176C [6]. Currently, researchers have bee

were cured at temperature of 60 ± 1 /C176C and 90 ± 5% relative humidity for 28 days. All compressive strength samples were removed from the steel moulds after 2 days and curing was continued. The compressive strength of samples for 3, 7 and 28 days was measured in trip- licate in according with BS EN 196-1:2005(E). The samples tested in compression after 3, 7 and 28 days without being exposed to elevated temperatures. The 28 days called reference samples, whilst those exposed to elevated temperatures were transferred to an oven for drying at 105 ± 2 /C176C for 24 h. After drying, the specimens were placed in a furnace and heated to 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 /C176C at a rate of 6.67 /C176C per minute and kept for 2 h period. After ﬁring, specimens were then left to cool gradually to room temperature. The residual compressive strength was mea- sured in triplicate. The thermal shock resistance were determined using the same procedure that other authors [40,41] used by heating the specimens (six specimens from each mix) after 28 days of curing for 40 min at 800 /C176C followed by quenching in water for 5 min. Such cycles are repeated until the samples are broken, damaged or deteriorated. In order to measure the pH of all mixes before and after exposure to elevated temperature, powder from center of the hardened cubes was extracted, grinded and mixed with distilled water (1 part powder, 10 parts water). These powder/ water mixes were kept rotating on a roller table for 24 h after which time they were placed in a centrifuge operating at 5000 rpm for 5 min to separate the solids from the liquid. The liquid was removed to a new bottle where the pH was measured using a hand held pH probe. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Compressive strength Generally, the reactivity and strength development of ﬂy ash depend on various factors such as composition, glass content, par- ticle size distribution, activator type and alkali content. Fig. 2 illustrates the compressive strength development results for OPC and AAFA pastes. The compressive strength of all pastes in- creased with increasing the hydration time, the OPC paste giving superior compressive strength. The compressive strength of AAFA pastes appeared to be highly dependent on activation concentra- tion. Increasing the sodium silicate concentration led to a signiﬁ- cant increase in the reactivity of the material [42] thus increasing in compressive strength at all ages as shown in Fig. 3 . The current results seemed to be similar with the results that were reported in the literature where many authors have reported that increasing activator concentration leads to an increase in mechan- ical strength of alkali-activated cementitious materials [43–51]. However, after 3, 7 and 28 days hydration, the compressive strength of M3 was 3.97, 3.78 and 2.51 higher than those of M2 respectively. Similarly, the compressive strength of M4 was 24.17, 94.95 and 7.676 higher than those of M2 after the same peri- ods of hydration. 3.2. The response of paste mixtures to thermal loading Exposing cementitious materials to elevated temperatures sig- niﬁcantly affect the internal structure stability. The behaviour of temperature exposure to 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 /C176C thermal loads was investigated by determining the residual strengths after ﬁring. The residual compressive strengths after cooling were deter- mined by an unstressed compression test [52]. This method gives lower values of compressive strength as comp

3.2.2.2. Residual compressive strength. Paste mixtures made with al- kali-activated ﬂy ash do not contain portlandite and expected to show better performance after exposure to elevated temperatures than hardened OPC pastes. Figs. 7 and 8 illustrate the residual and relative residual compressive strength results of AAFA made with three different concentrations of sodium silicate at 28 days and after exposure to elevated temperatures ranging from 200 /C176Cu p to 1000 /C176C with an increment 200 /C176C. Figs. 7 and 8 depict the strength of AAFA increased as temperature increased. The mixture with lower concentration of activator (M2) attaining its maximum strength of 36.29 MPa at 1000 /C176C. Subsequently, its reference strength was observed to increase gradually for the remainder to heating regime. The relative residual compressive strengths were 369.65%, 469.7%, 489.2%, 523.14% and 665.92% after exposure to 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 /C176C respectively. Evidently, as shown in Figs. 7 and 8 the same trend was observed for M3 where com- pressive strengths increased with the increasing in heating regime and achieving its maximum strength of 40.27 MPa after ﬁring at 1000 /C176C. The recorded relative strengths were 165.37%, 219.6%, 211.51%, 221.64% and 258.6% after exposure to 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 /C176C respectively. The mixture with higher concentra- tion of activator (M4) attaining a peak strength of 65.82 MPa at 200 /C176C. Subsequently, this strength was observed to decrease grad- ually for the remainder to heating regime. Strength of 52.95 MPa was recorded after 600 /C176C temperature exposure, which was still 11.98% improvement over the strength of the reference specimens. The ﬁnal strength of 33.35 MPa was recorded after 1000 /C176C tem- perature exposure which lower than the initial strength. The rela- tive residual compressive strengths were 139.2%, 127.68%, 111.98%, 90.37% and 70.53% after exposure to 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 /C176C respectively. The main feature of Figs. 7 and 8 is that the initial (reference) compressive strengths increased as activator concentration increased. On the contrary, the relative residual compressive strengths after exposure to elevated temperatures in- creased as activator concentration of sodium silicate decreased. Comparing the relative residual compressive strengths of AAFA that plotted in Fig. 8 with those of OPC that shown in Fig. 5 , its clearly noted that AAFA system have higher relative residual com- pressive strengths where the strength of OPC specimens dropped considerably between temperatureres of 400 /C176C and 800 /C176C. This strength deterioration is attributed to the Ca(OH) 2 decomposition that occurs at about 400 /C176C [56]. 3.2.2.3. Crystalline phases. An XRD technique was used to acquire a better understanding of the possible transformations underway in the original material as well as the samples subjected to elevated temperatures. Figs. 9 and 10 present XRD traces of the samples prepared with higher and lower concentrations of waterglass be- fore and after exposure to elevated temperatures. The FA activation with lower and higher concentrations of activator did not show the formation of new crystalline products compared to starting mate- rial. The quartz and mullite in the initial ash were affected by acti- vator concentrations or exposure to elevated temperatures. Peaks due to quartz, mullite and hematite of the crystalline component of ﬂy ash can be seen in addit

made with lower concentration of sodium silicate, while it was twenty-two cycles for the hardened AAFA pasted made with mod- erate concentration of sodium silicate. This may be related to, as the activator increased as the vapour pressure increased during heating. 3.2.4. pH values Generally, in construction it is important to maintain a high pH to form a passive protective layer on the surface of reinforcing steel and reduce corrosion attack. The FA used had a pH value of approx- imately 8.2 when mixed with water. However, the pH value of the pastes resulting from activation of FA with sodium silicate solu- tions appeared to be dependent on the concentration of the activa- tor. Fig. 16 shows the pH of the reference samples and the samples after heating of 200 /C176C, 400 /C176C, 600 /C176C and 800 /C176C. Evidently, a de- crease in pH values occurred with an increase in exposure temper- ature regardless of activator concentration. Furthermore, increasing the activator concentration led to an increase in pH in both cases reference and heated samples. Fig. 16 also illiterates that the reference OPC sample had the highest value of pH which slightly decreased after exposure to elevated temperatures. The pH of the AAFA samples started lower than those of OPC and de- creased faster after exposure to elevated temperatures reaching a value of approximately 11 after heating at 800 /C176C. Although the pH values of the AAFA pastes were lower than those of OPC pastes and decrease with the increase in elevated temperature, the alka- linity was still high enough to maintain a passive layer on the sur- face of steel reinforcement even up to 800 /C176C. 4. Conclusions The results reported in this investigation provide new data on ﬁre performance and thermal shock of AAFA pastes chemically activated with different concentrations of sodium silicate versus traditional Portland cement pastes. The experimental and analyti- cal studies have led to the following conclusions: 1. AAFA is more resistant to elevated temperatures than tradi- tional Portland cement. The relative residual strengths, for all AAFA pastes superior than traditional Portland cement system after exposure to 200 /C176C, 400 /C176C, 600 /C176C, 800 /C176C and 1000 /C176C. AAFA system appears more useful than OPC as a ﬁre resistant binder. It has therefore been established that FA based for alkali activation can be fabricated for construction purposes and these materials have great potential for ﬁre resistance applications. 2. Activator concentration has a marked inﬂuence on both the ini- tial strength and the residual strength after heating. 3. As the concentration of activator increased as the compressive strength increased. On the contrary, as the activator concentra- tion increased as the relative residual compressive strength after ﬁring decreased. For higher concentration (M4) the rela- tive residual strengths were 139.2%, 127.68%, 111.98%, 90.37% and 70.53% after exposure to 200 /C176C, 400 /C176C, 600 /C176C, 800 /C176C and 1000 /C176C respectively, while they were 369.65%, 469.7%, 489.2%, 523.14 and 665.92% for lower concentration (M2) related to their references. 4. The initial strength for the higher activator concentration was 47.28 MPa while the residual strength after exposure to 1000 /C176C was 33.35 MPa. One the other hand, the initial strength for M2 (lower activator concentration) and M3 (moderate acti- vator concentration) were 5.45 MPa and 15.57 MPa respectively while th

ence, as shown in Figs. 4 and 5 . The relative residual compressive strength was 121.45% referred its original before exposure to ele- vated temperature At 400 /C176C, the gain of strengths after heating 200 /C176C were lost but the original compressive strength partially af- fected by 400 /C176C, where the relative residual compressive strength was 99.7% related to its original before exposure to elevated tem- perature. At 600 /C176C, the retained strength was only 69.5% this is due to reduction of the Ca(OH) 2 content. At 800 /C176C and 1000 /C176C the related strengths dramatically decreased (due to decomposi- tion of CSH) to 30.2% and 11% respectively. 3.2.2. Hardened AAFA 3.2.2.1. Qualitative observations. The specimens (without ﬁring) with lower concentration of activator seemed to have grey colour lighter than those of higher activator concentration ( Fig. 6 ). After exposure to elevated temperature up (800 /C176C), there appeared to be a slight change from grey to pink. Increasing the temperature exposure to 1000 /C176C led to completely change the specimens col- our to darken pink colour. The rate of the darken increases in spec- imens with lower activator concentration than those of higher activator concentration ( Fig. 6 ). The AAFA paste samples did not have any cracks on the surface after the temperature exposure. Fig. 5. Relative residual compressive strength of OPC pastes exposed to elevated temperature. Fig. 6. Photographs of hardened AAFA pastes activated with lower and higher concentration of activators before and after exposure to 800 /C176C and 1000 /C176C. A.M. Rashad, S.R. Zeedan / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 3098–3107 3101

better than Portland cement mortars in reinforcement steel bond- ing [17]. AAFA can protect steel reinforcement from corrosion [18], can be used to ﬁx toxic elements [19–23], the dry shrinkage of AAFA binders can be lower than that of Portland cement binders [24–27]. García-Lodeiro et al. [28] reported that when applying standard C1260-94 found that 8 M NaOH activated FA mortars ex- panded less than OPC and lower creep than conventional concrete [29]. Moreover, the production of alkali activated materials does not require a high level of energy consumption. The energy con- sumption is calculated to be approximately 60% less than that re- quired by Portland cement [30]. Certain key properties, including performance when exposed to elevated temperatures, have received very little attention unlike the ﬁre performance of Portland cement system. In Portland ce- ment pastes, strength loss above 50% when exposed to tempera- tures between 400 and 500 /C176C is associated with dehydroxylation of hydroxide and continuous dehydration of the calcium silicate hydrate (CSH) from 105 /C176C [31]. Fly ash blended Portland cement pastes have performed better at these temperatures than Portland cement alone since the ﬂy ash reduced the amount of Ca(OH) 2 in the binder following hydration [32,33]. In short, it is clear that enormous efforts were made throughout the 20th century to im- prove Portland cement concrete technology. At the same time, however, very limited research was conducted on new binders able to provide technical alternatives to conventional cement that could be produced at a fraction of energy cost and environmental impact. There are limited publications regarding the ﬁre performance of alkali-activated ﬂy ash compared to OPC system. The available re- searches can be summarized in the following: Fly ash-based geo- polymers are generally believed to perform better than Portland cement concretes in ﬁre, due to their ceramic-like properties [34]. Kong et al. [35] reported that strength of ﬂy ash-based geo- polymer increased after elevated temperature exposure. Guerrieri and Sanjayan [36] studied the behaviour of combined ﬂy ash/ slag-based geopolymers when exposed to high temperatures. The combined ﬂy ash and slag at ratios of 100/0, 65/35, 50/50, 35/65 and 0/100 (wt.%). The activators used have different molecular sil- icate modulus ratios with sodium (Na) dosages of 4 and 8 (wt.%). They concluded that the initial strength increased, the residual strength exponentially decreased. Kong et al. [35] studied the AAFA activated with sodium silicate and potassium hydroxide solutions when exposed to elevated temperature versus alkali activated metakaolin. They concluded that, the strength of the AAFA in- creased after exposure to elevated temperatures (800 /C176C). Kong and Sanjayan [37] studied the effect of elevated temperatures on AAFA paste, mortar and concrete. They activated FA by sodium sil- icate and potassium hydroxide solutions. They concluded that, aggregate size larger than 10 mm resulted in good strength perfor- mances in both ambient and elevated temperatures. Kong and San- jayan [38] studied damage behaviour of alkali-activated ﬂy ash with sodium silicate solution/potassium hydroxide composites ex- posed to elevated temperature. The results indicated that AAFA exhibited strength increased of about 53% after temperature expo- sure. AAFA/aggregate composites with identical AAFA binder for- mulations decreased in strength by up to 65%

crystallizes fairly quickly and easily [57,58]. In addition, this zeo- lite, found relatively frequently in alkali-activated ﬂy ash systems [59,60] maintains its thermal stability up to 600 /C176C. It is also char- acterized by a propensity to undergo minor dehydration and recrystallization as nepheline (NaAlSiO 4), albite (NaAlSi 3O8), or other stable anhydrous aluminosilicates. Its aluminosilicate struc- ture, which helps maintain the dimensional stability of the mate- rial [58] is unaffected by this change. 3.2.2.4. Thermogravimetric analysis. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) was used to assess weight loss during controlled heating of the sample in order to identify phases present [61]. The weight loss and derivative weight loss curves in the range 0–1000 /C176C were obtained for the M2 and M4 after 28 days hydration ( Fig. 11). The sharp decrease in weight before 200 /C176C as recorded in the TGA thermogram. After the initial rapid decline the weight loss of the tested samples were stabilized between 200 /C176C and 1000 /C176C. Anyhow, the main DTG peak centered at approximately 100 /C176C is caused by the loss of adsorbed and com- bined water in the N-A-S-H gel [62,63]. No weight loss or DTG peak was presented for Ca(OH) 2 (usually centered at approximately 450 /C176C). Fig. 11 shows the DTG peaks centered at approximately 100 /C176C are greater for M4 than those of M2 indicating that more hydration has occurred with higher activator concentration. 3.2.2.5. Microstructural analysis. Investigations of the microstruc- ture of the hardened pastes using SEM showed distinct changes in morphology as a consequence of exposure to elevated tempera- tures and concentration of activator. Fig. 12 shows SEM micro- graphs of fracture surface of the hardened AAFA pastes have lower and higher activator concentration at 28 days hydration and after exposure to 600 /C176C and 1000 /C176C. The microstructure of the AAFA with lower activator concentration at 28 days is shown in ( Fig. 12 a). This sample appeared to have a large proportion of un-reacted particles and the materials seemed to be heteroge- neous. Furthermore, a glass-like matrix with imbedded ﬂy ash spheres was present. After exposure to 600 /C176C the microstructure was transformed into a matrix more reacted material with crystal- line reaction products ( Fig. 12b). Further increasing in temperature up to 1000 /C176C led to sintering reactions of un-reacted ﬂy ash parti- cles (Fig. 12c). Fig. 12d shows a specimen prepared with the higher concentration of sodium silicate where the microstructure is glassy and denser than those shown in Fig. 12 a. After exposure to 600 /C176C the microstructure seemed to more reactive than those shown in Fig. 12 d. Further increasing in temperature treatment up to 1000 /C176C, a signiﬁcant increase in porosity was observed ( Fig. 12 f). Figs. 13 and 14 show EDS trace of spots marked 1, 2, 3 and 4 in Fig. 12. Fig. 13 illiterates the EDS of spot 1 in Fig. 12a which repre- sents the un-reacted FA particles in hardened AAFA pastes acti- vated with lower concentration of activator. Fig. 14a–c shows the EDS trace of the spots marked 2, 3 and 4 in Fig. 12a, c and f respec- tively (main matrix) is mostly alumnosilicate gel for which the EDS image shows the dominant peaks for Al and Si.
